[Pasted text #39 +3 lines]
If you are right, then why do we have such a severe regression in those 4 pieces?
So all of the improvement is in the SER detection, not actual accuracy improvement?
ARG. If we were greenfielding this, how would we compare the 2 xml files for accuracy?
Are we looking at any other elements for accuracy besides notes?
But we are only measuring the accuracy of the note placement. Nothing else. What if everything is borked?
What methodology does the main branch use for determining SER?
OUR eval scripts live outside the repo. They were evaluating before we came on board. How did previous developers calculate SER in main?

It seems to me that for our purposes, checking that the xml files are close to each other is most stable way to know if the project works. I propose that we rewrite the analysis to include sequence comparison. I think the measure comparison is helpful, but fragile in it's current state. If, rather than comparing  m-0 to m-0 we compared measures in order, IE if gt starts at 0 and our output begins at 1, compare 0 to 1. I think we need accuracy measures on everything that makes this up...Key signature, Time signature, Clefs, Slur count, Grace note count, Staccato count and Repeats and anything else we can think of that would affect accuracy.
I like it. Define structural errors.
I would like to measure accuracy on each of these. Ties and slurs seem important. The number and placement accuracy should be taken into account.
Yes. Numbers of measures, but also measure accuracy, flexible enough to pick our pickup measures and not destroy the accuracy of the whole piece.
Stop
You don't build until we are done planning. What the hell
measure accuracy would check all notes in the grandstaff accross a measure or only per staff?

This would be useful as a way to isolate errors. Moreso that than as a metric, but I think it needs to be included anyway. "Everthing is good to the middle of the second page...measure 18", but it has to be flexible enough to see that the transformer injected a bar that it shouldn't have or omitted one. 100% perfect measures is also helpful as a metric too.
Push back.

Strict positioning comparison is helpful if you have some accurate measures before the error to know roughly where we went off track, but if that error ocurrs in the first measure, it ceases to be helpful. Does sequence SER recognize an insertion/deletion as such and then correct comparisions to accommodate, or is it borked afer an insertion/deletion
That's better. Let's do it for now.
This all exists in the homr-eval?
I just made homr-eval a git repo. Will you commit whate we have right now to a feature branch
Yes, .gitignore. I'd say ignore builds, runs, spot and logs? What do you think?
okay
I'd say ignore them. We can easily regenerate right?
I've pushed it. Go ahead and create a new branch and build it
Let's test the eval quickly. YOu can probably do that in your env since it is only comparing xml files?
Can we use this to compare the main?
Well, fuck
Let's finish building the tools to view the data. Also wire this in so we can run it against whatever new runs we make.

i need this to be bigger in scope that just how we fix staff-cropping. We need to have a plan for researching and testing different theories to improve our outputs. I'm leaning toward a ralph loop integrating 2 different agents...claude and codex. We could even loop in gemini if that would be useful?

I'm shooting for a collaborative/antagonistic research environment. The eval process is so heave/time-consuming that we need to limit the number of times we run it for the whole data set and the agents cannot run it themselves. For now this will need to be a theory only, modify code and then a manual run in the host. I want to find patterns across the test set that will yield general gains rather that spot improvements. We are using the 69 pieces for accuracy test, but hope improvements will be seen across all existing music pieces.

I'm betting on the notion that different agents will diverge the solution set, and that back and forth within a cycle could possibly refine the results. I kind of like the idea of a "turn" being some diagnostics, some adversarial and proposals. Assigning roles at the outset to specific agents pigeon-holes in a way that I don't want to start with. We could write "assume the roles of {role:Daignostic agent, adversarial agent} and do {related task}"

Let's define the cycle better and then determine how many cycles should run before returning to the human for analysis, at which point I may run the eval or say keep working on it. I think shared-state docs are the right way to go: things we have tried/suggested/analyzed, what worked, what didn't. Key is we launch an agent every time with a good understanding of where we are and documentation that can be reviewed if necessary.
I like it. How do we determine which agent takes which stage, or do they all take a turn?
Okay, but what about: a cycle is a turn for each agent. Each agent diagnoses, proposes, critiques existing proposals and the cycles continue until all 3 agree on a proposal.

Yes. For the record, I have no way currently to automate this, so it will be a manual process for me while we test. I'll start a specific agent in it's container, and tell it to run through the process, then the next agent. I'll try to mix up order. The first agent to run will have less to do, but as we build on what we have, we should get more. Another aspect that we need to address is the need fo more data. The agents will all have access to previous eval runs with statistical data, but they may need the debug images or some logging to figure out what is happening at a certain point in the process. I would rather that they stop the loop and ask for a debugging run than continue guessing.
okay
I like this. It seems sound.
This looks good to me. Yes write the state doc. Should this be a local command, the same for all agents?
I would like slash commands where possible for the agents.
All agents use the same container environment
Should we duplicate to .codex and .gemini?
I'll have them duplicate on their first run. Let's continue
Yes. I can even make the decision to follow a single agent-endorsed path after the 3 cycles.
